content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 19, 1990


Honorable Ross Johnson
Assemblymember, 64th Assembly District
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA  94249-0001

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice
                                            Our File No. I-90-253

Dear Assemblymember Johnson:

     This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you by this office concerning the
propriety of using campaign funds to pay for generic voter
registration and absentee ballot programs by a political party
under the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/  The issue of which expenditures are considered
associated with a candidate's office was considered at the July
Commission meeting which delayed issuance of this letter.  Since
your advice request seeks general guidance with respect to your
question, we are treating your request as one for informal as-
sistance.2/

                            QUESTIONS

     1.  May an elected official or candidate contribute campaign
funds to a political party for generic voter registration activity
which is designated for a district other than the official's own?

     2.  May an elected official or candidate contribute campaign
funds to a political party for absentee ballot programs which are
designated for a district other than the official's own?


_________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.  Sec-
tions 85800-85807, which describe the permissible uses of campaign
funds, are referred to as the "personal use" laws.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.  (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-253
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. An elected official or candidate may not contribute
campaign funds to a political party for generic voter registration
activity which is specifically designated for a district other
than the official's own. However, campaign funds may be
contributed to a political party for generic voter registration
activity in the official's district or statewide.

2. An elected official or candidate may not contribute
campaign funds to a political party for absentee ballot programs
which are specifically designated for a district other than the
official's own. However, campaign funds may be contributed to a
political party for absentee ballot programs in the official's
district or statewide.

DISCUSSION

This letter confirms that your letter dated March 27, 1990,
accurately summarizes the Commission's current position on the use
of campaign funds for voter registration. Section 85201(c)
requires candidates for public office to deposit all campaign
contributions they receive into a campaign bank account.
Section 85202(b) states:

All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office for which the
candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200,
that he or she intends to seek or expenses associ-
ated with holding that office.

Sections 85201(c) and 85202(b) read together require that any
expenditure of campaign funds be associated with a candidate's
campaign for the office for which the funds were raised.
Registering voters of a candidate's party affiliation in the
candidate's jurisdiction and encouraging voting through absentee
ballot programs are clearly related to the candidate's ability to
seek and hold office. Thus, contributing campaign funds to a
political party or political committee for these purposes is
consistent with the trust.3/ (Martin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301,


3/ Payments for voter registration must be made from the
candidate's account for a future campaign. (Regulation 18525,
copy enclosed.)

Our File No. I-90-253
Page 3


copy enclosed.)4/

However, the Commission has consistently interpreted the
trust provision as precluding a candidate from using campaign
funds for communications which predominantly benefit other
candidates. (Milman Advice letter, No. I-90-206.) Consequently,
a candidate may not make an expenditure for voter registration
outside his jurisdiction.5/ This limitation is consistent with
Section 85304, which prohibits candidates from transferring
campaign funds to other candidates, and other Commission advice
concerning the use of campaign funds. (Dowell Advice Letter,
No. A-89-566, copy enclosed.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:plh

Enclosures


4/ In reaching this conclusion, the Commission staff is cognizant
of Regulation 18535 (copy enclosed). This regulation sets forth
the requirements under which political parties and political
action committees may receive contributions not subject to the
Act's contribution limits. (Sections 85302 and 85303(c).) Under
Section 85304 and Commission advice consistent with this section,
we do not interpret this regulation to permit candidates to
earmark and transfer campaign funds as described above. In
reaching this conclusion, the Commission staff is cognizant of our
advice in the Martin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301 (copy enclosed)
in which we advised that a candidate can transfer funds in
unlimited amounts to a political party as long as the fund were to
be used "for purposes other than making contributions to
candidates." We indicated in that letter that the commission had
not yet defined the phrase "for purposes other than making
contributions to candidates." Thus, we see no conflict between
our conclusions in the Martin letter and this letter. However, to
the extent that this advice letter conflicts with the Martin
Advice Letter, the current letter controls.

5/ Where a contribution is made to a state party organization for
a statewide program of voter registration, we would presume the
primary benefit of the candidate's contribution is obtained by the
candidate absent facts to the contrary.
